body_txt: Bullets are whistling, shells are exploding, panicked soldiers dash here and there splattered with mud and blood, the air is thick with shouted commands and the rich stench of death! And yet our hero is smoking a pipe , sipping his tea , and doing the Times Crossword Puzzle , for he is an Imperturbable Englishman. He is like The Stoic , but an Imperturbable Englishman adds style to his stoicism by his exaggerated disregard for danger and hardship. Not only does he cope with them: he cheerily dismisses them. No matter the disaster, he always keeps his rationality and composure. This is known as having a Stiff Upper Lip: "Keep a stiff upper lip, chaps!", "Buck up! Stiff upper lip and all that!", etc. Ironically, the term "stiff upper lip" is actually American in origin. In Britain today, the phrase is only used ironically to invoke the trope, but the concept is very real: witness the "Keep Calm and Carry On" poster, a more laconic version of the picture on this page, which adorns many British office walls. In terms of Dialogue , Understatement is key; this is why the RAF Ace Pilot is the poster child for Major Injury Underreaction . Stress is met with Dissonant Serenity , grief with Angst? What Angst? , and unbridled joy with a curt "Jolly good". When most people imagine the “stiff upper lip” in action, they imagine someone calm and composed in the face of danger, maintaining a sense of normalcy amid chaos. This form of British stoicism is often portrayed as meeting crisis with quiet nonchalance, carrying on with everyday routines as a subtle display of resilience, especially in wartime. More broadly, it reflects a cultural ideal of remaining controlled under pressure, valuing discipline over outward displays of emotion. British stoicism can apply to professions and roles where someone must be clear-headed and a competent decision-maker, where emotional control, steadiness, and restraint are essential to success in high-stress environments or fields of work that require a strict adherence to discipline and regulations. In Victorian England , British stoicism was a cultural ideal because it was believed that emotional restraint was a mark of masculinity. Victorian morality valued status, reputation, and respectability. Within this culture, overt displays of emotion were seen as weakness or even a form of savagery. Within The British Empire , soldiers were expected to follow their duties, be resilient, and restrain their emotions as a hallmark of Imperial prestige while in foreign countries. Self-control and self-mastery were believed to make British soldiers appear impervious and intimidating. Compare Gentleman Snarker , Trying Not to Cry , This Is No Time to Panic , Quintessential British Gentleman , and The Jeeves . East Asian equivalents may include the Japanese cultural concept of " It Can't Be Helped " and more positive portrayals of the Inscrutable Oriental . This is what someone who has or pretends to have Nerves of Steel would supposedly look like from the outside. Often crosses over with Brits Love Tea , as stopping everything to make a cup of tea and consider the situation when faced by danger is both Funny , Badass , and, as seen below happens surprisingly often in real life. Not to be confused with While Rome Burns , when the response is denial instead of steely resolve, or Apathetic Citizens , which replaces resolve with... well, apathy. See British Stuffiness for more negative portrayals of this British trope. Lie Back and Think of England arises when this trait gets in the way of a relationship. One of the Stock British Phrases .                    Non-British examples British pre- World War I British during World War I and Interwar British during World War II British post World War II
